who mounts the witness stand
and tells of himself as for years a
base and willing tool in the hands
of his alleged master. Haywood;
how ne obeyed the most brutal or
ders, and carried out the most
frightful outrages, treated always
with contumely and contempt and
showered with denunciation and
curses.


Even the most biased believers in
the guilt of Haywood admit that it
is almost impossible to conceive
that this Orchard could possibly
have been the man he describes
himself.


Wingate, one of the talesmen,
willing enough to serve, declared
that he could not under anycircum-
stances believe Orchard upon oath.
The confession was too horrible;
the transformation was too incon-
ceivable.


1t is on account of these irrecon-
cilable aspects of the man Orchard
that Haywood's friends persist in
accusing the mine owners and the
unfriendly powers of the law of a
conspiracy to deliberately fasten
guilt upon an innocent man and
wreck the Western Federation.


They say, if Orchard is the
abominable and constituencies
criminal he confessed himself, how
can his oath be taken? If he, in his
years of manhood, had fallen to the
unutterable depths OF degradation
he describes, how this marvellous
alteration in character? II the
photograph of Orchard the criminal
shows him as he is. why the presen-
tation before the court and before
the public of this well-dressed, we'll
groomed man, who is so eager to
again recite his crimes for the sake
of "consciencee"?


1t is for the sake OF immunity,
they declare. Also that Orchard is,
no doubt, despicable and criminal,
that he is undoubtedly guilty Of
many Of the crimes to which he has
confessed, but that the all-powerful
influence of money, aided by hostile
officialdom, has assured him no lit
tie degree of immunity, provided he
acts out his disgusting characterize
ton to the disgraceful end.
